sources: 5x RegulatorWatchdog CSV inventory (30 regulators, BIS-anchored, conservative)
3x LLM-drafted regulator tables (richer specifics, partially unverified)
provenance.rule: Where the article conflicts with drafts, the article wins. Draft-only specifics flagged needs-verification.

regulators.key_framework: OECD AI Principles
regulators.implications: Widely used baseline for trustworthy AI (robustness, transparency, accountability, human-centred values); cross-border policy anchor for enterprise AI governance.

regulators.key_framework: Sound Practices for Responsible Adoption of AI; Financial Stability Implications of AI
regulators.implications: Pushes institution-wide AI governance across the AI lifecycle, board/senior-management focus, third-party/vendor dependence, and financial-stability vulnerabilities.

frameworks.notes: Practice count and 2026 consultation status cited only in draft - confirm against FSB primary source.

regulators.key_framework: BIS/FSI work on regulating AI in finance; BCBS work on AI/ML, model risk, operational resilience, third-party risk
regulators.implications: Treats AI as an extension of prudential governance and model risk management - explainability, validation, data governance, accountability in core banking use cases.

frameworks.notes: Cited only in an LLM draft; no confirmation a numbered 'BCBS 350' exists - verify before ingestion.

regulators.key_framework: Work on AI in capital markets and conduct/market-integrity implications
regulators.implications: Relevant for broker-dealers, exchanges, asset managers using AI in advice, surveillance, market-abuse detection, investor comms; expect conduct, transparency, market-integrity controls.

regulators.key_framework: Insurance supervisory work applying AI governance, model and consumer-protection expectations
regulators.implications: Important for bancassurance/insurance affiliates - underwriting fairness, explainability, governance, customer outcomes.

regulators.key_framework: AI use in AML/CFT under risk-based compliance frameworks
regulators.implications: Supports AI-enabled surveillance and fraud detection but requires explainable controls, data quality, and oversight where AI influences suspicious-activity monitoring.

regulators.key_framework: EU AI Act (Reg. (EU) 2024/1689) plus sectoral financial rules
regulators.implications: High-risk AI use cases require stronger controls on data governance, human oversight, logging, monitoring, documentation; map use cases against both AI Act and sector rules.

frameworks.notes: Prohibited practices and high-risk obligations phase in through 2026-2027; credit scoring + life/health insurance pricing are high-risk.
Referenced by APRA as the de facto recognised control frameworks; cross-jurisdiction relevance.

regulators.key_framework: Existing prudential governance, model risk, outsourcing, operational resilience applied to AI
regulators.implications: Significant institutions should expect AI reviewed through governance, internal controls, and model-risk lenses rather than as a standalone carve-out.

regulators.key_framework: AI Update (2024); existing rules apply (Consumer Duty, SM&CR, operational resilience); sandbox/innovation pathways
regulators.implications: Firms remain accountable under conduct, Consumer Duty, operational resilience and SM&CR-style governance even when using third-party or embedded AI tools.

frameworks.notes: Principles-based; existing rules apply (Consumer Duty, SM&CR, op resilience).

regulators.key_framework: SS1/23 Model Risk Management Principles and operational resilience applied to AI/ML
regulators.implications: Prudentially important firms should classify many AI systems as model-risk-bearing, requiring validation, controls, clear ownership.

frameworks.notes: Extends model-risk discipline to AI for PRA-regulated firms.

regulators.key_framework: AI and data protection guidance
regulators.implications: Any AI using personal data needs lawful basis, transparency, minimisation, and governance around automated decision-making and fairness.

regulators.key_framework: SR 26-2 (Apr 2026) Model Risk Management - supersedes SR 11-7; safety-and-soundness expectations extended to AI/ML
regulators.implications: Treat AI systems used for material decisions as governed models requiring validation, change management, bias review, vendor oversight. SR 26-2 explicitly extends MRM discipline to AI/ML.

frameworks.notes: Supersedes SR 11-7; explicitly extends model-risk discipline to AI/ML.
Historic MRM baseline; superseded by SR 26-2.

regulators.key_framework: Guidance on AI in investment advice / broker-dealer activity; Predictive Data Analytics proposal; AI-washing enforcement focus
regulators.implications: Broker-dealers/advisers may need to identify and neutralise AI-related conflicts in investor interactions; AI marketing claims must be supportable.

frameworks.notes: Proposal status/scope shifts; confirm current status (proposed vs withdrawn vs final) before relying on it.

regulators.key_framework: AI topic hub; Regulatory Notice 24-09; guidance on AI in customer-facing communications
regulators.implications: Treats AI as subject to existing technology-neutral rules - communications, supervision, surveillance, suitability-style processes, third-party tools.

frameworks.notes: Confirms FINRA rules are technology-neutral and apply to AI/embedded tools.

regulators.key_framework: Existing consumer financial protection and automated-decisioning scrutiny
regulators.implications: AI affecting retail credit, servicing or chatbots can trigger UDAAP, adverse-action, fair-lending and transparency concerns.

regulators.key_framework: Unfair/deceptive practices, automated decision-making, AI claims
regulators.implications: AI claims, explainability, fairness and deceptive product statements create enforcement exposure, especially for client-facing features and marketing.

regulators.key_framework: AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0, Jan 2023)
regulators.implications: Not a financial regulator but a strong control taxonomy for mapping policy, testing, monitoring and governance requirements into an enterprise framework.

frameworks.notes: Govern / Map / Measure / Manage; widely used as control taxonomy.

regulators.key_framework: FEAT principles; Veritas methodology; AI Risk Management Guidelines (consultation Nov 2025, finalisation expected 2026); AI Verify; Notice FSM-N20
regulators.implications: Strong benchmark for fairness, ethics, accountability, transparency in financial AI; more prescriptive than UK/AU; mandatory inventory and lifecycle controls proposed.

frameworks.notes: Fairness, Ethics, Accountability, Transparency; Veritas + AI Verify provide testing tools.

regulators.key_framework: High-level AI principles; consumer protection / regtech / supervisory expectations
regulators.implications: Important for chatbot, regtech, fraud and operational AI deployments; emphasis on governance and implementation discipline.

regulators.key_framework: Existing conduct and algorithmic controls applied to AI in markets; GenAI language-model circular
regulators.implications: Securities firms should expect AI oversight in trading, client communications, surveillance and outsourcing governance.

regulators.key_framework: Generative AI, recommendation algorithms, deep synthesis, and sector supervision
regulators.implications: One of the more prescriptive regimes - content, security, algorithm filing and sector controls that can materially affect model deployment architecture.

regulators.key_framework: Existing financial regulation plus AI governance guidance references
regulators.implications: Typically principles-based, but material AI use still raises governance, outsourcing and explainability expectations.

frameworks.notes: Specific 48-hour notification and Q3-2026 timing appear only in a draft - confirm.

regulators.key_framework: APRA 30 Apr 2026 AI industry letter (expects ISO/IEC 42001 & 23894, integrated assurance); CPS 230, CPS 234, CPG 234 (extended to AI), CPS 220; ASIC 8 May 2026 cyber-resilience letter
regulators.implications: Most explicit prudential statement on AI to date. Integrated assurance across cyber, data governance, model performance, operational resilience, privacy, conduct. AI governance failures treated as licence/duty-of-care issues.

frameworks.notes: Expects ISO/IEC 42001 & 23894 and integrated assurance across six risk categories.
AI may be a material service / material model.
CPG 234 practice guide now extended to AI.
Frames frontier AI as part of directors' duty of care.

regulators.key_framework: Emerging AI governance in finance noted in comparative supervisory work
regulators.implications: Relevant for regional banking operations; likely focus is governance, outsourcing, data handling, prudent deployment.

regulators.key_framework: Financial free-zone AI/ML guidance plus national AI governance developments
regulators.implications: Important for cross-border hubs using AI in wealth, markets and compliance tooling, especially outsourcing and data-transfer issues.

regulators.key_framework: AI governed through prudential, consumer, privacy and securities rules; OSFI Guideline E-23 (model risk)
regulators.implications: AI in banking and wealth products should be controlled through model risk, outsourcing, privacy and fair-outcomes frameworks.

frameworks.notes: Reported effective ~2027 and AI/ML explicitly in scope - confirm scope and effective date vs OSFI primary source.

regulators.key_framework: AI governance emerging alongside data protection (LGPD) and financial supervision
regulators.implications: Material for Latin America operations, especially customer data, model fairness and prudential accountability.

regulators.key_framework: Existing financial rules and data/privacy law applied to AI
regulators.implications: Relevant for conduct, model governance and outsourcing in bank and insurer use cases.

regulators.key_framework: Existing sectoral regulation plus digital/AI governance developments
regulators.implications: AI in lending, onboarding, fraud and capital markets raises explainability, outsourcing and fairness issues even absent a dedicated financial AI rulebook.

regulators.key_framework: Technology-neutral prudential and outsourcing supervision; AI risk-management guidance
regulators.implications: Important for cross-border wealth and banking operations; focus on accountability, model governance, data risk and resilience.

control_themes.description: Complete, current register of AI use cases with high/medium/low risk tiering and entity/business-line ownership.
control_themes.supporting_regulators_examples: EU AI Act, SR 26-2, APRA, MAS, BCBS, OSFI E-23

control_themes.description: AI treated as governed models: identification of scope, independent validation, change management, vendor oversight.
control_themes.supporting_regulators_examples: SR 26-2, PRA SS1/23, BCBS, ECB, MAS, OSFI

control_themes.description: Board-approved AI policy, named owners, attestation and escalation paths.
control_themes.supporting_regulators_examples: FCA SM&CR, SR 26-2, APRA, MAS, OSFI, FSB

control_themes.description: Defined methodology, demographic coverage, frequency and remediation for protected-attribute and proxy-discrimination risk.
control_themes.supporting_regulators_examples: EU AI Act, MAS FEAT, CFPB, ICO

control_themes.description: Model documentation/cards, explanation methods, consumer disclosure and automated-decision transparency.
control_themes.supporting_regulators_examples: EU AI Act, FINMA, OSFI, CFPB, ICO

control_themes.description: Lawful basis, lineage, minimisation, data-quality and protection across the AI lifecycle.
control_themes.supporting_regulators_examples: ICO, EU AI Act, BaFin/DORA, MAS

control_themes.description: Vendor due diligence, contract clauses, audit rights, exit planning and concentration monitoring for AI/cloud providers.
control_themes.supporting_regulators_examples: DORA, US interagency TPRM, MAS, APRA, OSFI, FSB

control_themes.description: Performance thresholds, drift alerts and periodic (e.g. quarterly) board reporting.
AI as material service/model under resilience regimes; BCM integration and testing.
Incident taxonomy, notification workflows and post-mortems aligned to each regulator's timelines.
Internal-audit AI coverage, external/independent assurance and inspection readiness.
Control of AI-washing, supportable AI claims and conduct in advice/communications.
control_themes.supporting_regulators_examples: SEC, FTC, FINRA, FCA Consumer Duty

verified_updates_2026.action_for_dev: Update key_framework + frameworks for REG-FED; SR 11-7 is now historic.
Most explicit prudential AI statement; expects ISO/IEC 42001 & 23894 + integrated assurance.
Frames frontier AI as directors' duty of care.
UK principles-based; existing rules apply; SS1/23 extends MRM to AI.
Use the regulation citation, not just 'EU AI Act'.
